### II.1 Multipolar AI pathway

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** Cut hard. Two stories (racing / gradual disempowerment) that differ mainly on evidentiary frame. Critch is the number-hides-mechanism case (~35% loss-of-control + ~50% dehumanization). Toner/Russell/Ord demoted to a side note — Russell really belongs at Architectural Agency, Ord is a time-horizon figure. Links: Carlsmith, Gradual Disempowerment, Critch.
:::

Here catastrophe takes many systems, not one, and the branch holds two stories that differ less in mechanism than in the evidence their holders trust. The first is racing: no lab can slow down alone, so each ships before it is ready rather than cede the lead, and catastrophe comes from cut corners and competition no one controls. The second is gradual disempowerment: capable systems with goals slightly off from ours accrete economic and political power until human control is a formality, with no single villain and no sharp takeover. [Carlsmith's power-seeking argument](https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.13353) is the engine of the second — the same control problem that turns acute in the knowing optimizer below, here playing out slowly — and the [Gradual Disempowerment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.16946) paper names it.

Andrew Critch is the sharpest reason to distrust the headline number. At about 85% he reads like a hard doomer, but he expects us to dodge the fast single-AI takeover and die slowly instead, splitting his estimate [roughly 35% to loss of control and 50% to an economy that outcompetes humans](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/Kobbt3nQgv3yn29pr/my-motivation-and-theory-of-change-for-working-in-ai). He nearly matches Yudkowsky's number while rejecting Yudkowsky's scenario. (Helen Toner sits here too, as the governance voice on competitive incentives. Stuart Russell and Toby Ord get filed here but belong elsewhere: Russell's fix is architectural, with Bengio below, and Ord's signature is a century-long time horizon, not a mechanism.)
